title: Delegated legislation
summary: 9084 — types, reasons for, and controls on delegated legislation.
learningObjectives: Define delegated legislation and its types.
Explain why Parliament delegates law-making power.

simpleExplanation.title: Law made outside Parliament
simpleExplanation.summary: Parliament grants ministers and bodies power to make detailed rules — faster and more flexible, but raises democratic accountability concerns.
steps: Types — Orders in Council, statutory instruments, bylaws.
Reasons — time, detail, local knowledge, emergencies.

flashcards.back: Most common DL — government ministers under enabling Act.
Made by Privy Council — emergencies, transfer of powers.
Local authorities/public bodies — e.g. parking restrictions.
SI becomes law unless rejected within 40 days.
Must be actively approved by Parliament before in force.
Beyond powers granted — courts can declare DL invalid.
An Act of Parliament that delegates law-making power to a non-parliamentary body, such as a government minister or a local authority. It sets the framework and limits for the delegated legislation.
A Latin term meaning 'beyond the powers'. In the context of delegated legislation, it means a law has been made that goes beyond the authority granted by the enabling Act. Such a law is void.

A parliamentary control where a piece of delegated legislation (usually a Statutory Instrument) must be actively approved by a vote in Parliament before it can become law. It is used for more significant powers.
The most common parliamentary control. A Statutory Instrument is laid before Parliament and will automatically become law after 40 days unless a motion to annul it is passed by either House.

flashcards.front: What was the legal principle from the Aylesbury Mushroom Case (1972)?
flashcards.back: This case is the leading authority for procedural ultra vires. The Minister failed to consult the Mushroom Growers' Association as required by the enabling Act, so the regulations were declared void in relation to them.

sections.content: **Delegated legislation** (1.1.3) — know **three types**, **controls** (parliamentary + judicial), and **procedural/substantive ultra vires**.

Delegated legislation, often called secondary legislation, is law created by bodies other than Parliament, under powers granted by an Act of Parliament known as an 'enabling' or 'parent' Act. There are three main types. Orders in Council are made by the Privy Council, typically in emergency situations when Parliament is not sitting, under the authority of the Emergency Powers Act 1920, or to give legal effect to EU directives (pre-Brexit). Statutory Instruments (SIs) are the most common form, made by government ministers within their specific departments to implement or amend existing legislation. For example, the Minister for Transport might use an SI to alter regulations on seatbelt use. Finally, By-laws are created by local authorities (e.g., county councils) or public corporations to address matters of local concern, such as parking restrictions or banning dogs from parks.

items: Delegated legislation is law made by non-parliamentary bodies using powers from an enabling Act.
Orders in Council are made by the Privy Council, often for emergencies.
Statutory Instruments are the most common type, made by government ministers.
By-laws are made by local authorities or public corporations for local matters.

sections.content: Parliament relies on delegated legislation for several crucial reasons. Primarily, it saves parliamentary time. Parliament lacks the time to debate every detailed rule required for a complex society, so enabling Acts provide a broad framework, leaving ministers to fill in the specifics. Secondly, it allows for the use of technical expertise. Modern legislation often covers complex and technical subjects, such as environmental protection or medical regulations, where specialist knowledge from relevant government departments is essential. Furthermore, it provides flexibility, as delegated legislation can be enacted or amended more quickly than an Act of Parliament, which is vital for responding to new situations or emergencies. Finally, it permits local knowledge to be utilised, as local councils can create by-laws tailored to the specific needs of their area, something Parliament cannot do effectively.

items: Saves Parliamentary Time: Parliament can focus on broad principles, not minor details.
Technical Expertise: Allows specialists in government departments to contribute.
Flexibility and Speed: Can be passed quickly to respond to changing circumstances.
Local Knowledge: Local authorities can create rules appropriate for their specific area.

sections.content: Parliament retains ultimate authority and exercises control over delegated legislation through several mechanisms. The most fundamental control is the enabling Act itself, which sets the precise limits on what powers are delegated, to whom, and for what purpose. Parliament can repeal or amend this Act at any time. Furthermore, most Statutory Instruments are subject to parliamentary procedure. The 'negative resolution' procedure means the SI becomes law unless rejected by Parliament within 40 days. The rarer 'affirmative resolution' procedure requires the SI to be expressly approved by Parliament before it can become law. Finally, the Joint Committee on Statutory Instruments (the 'Scrutiny Committee') reviews all SIs and can refer any problematic instrument to Parliament's attention, for example, if it goes beyond the powers granted by the parent Act (substantive ultra vires).

items: The Enabling Act: Defines the scope, purpose, and limits of the delegated power.
Affirmative Resolution: Requires express parliamentary approval before becoming law.
Negative Resolution: Becomes law automatically unless Parliament objects within 40 days.
Scrutiny Committee: Reviews SIs for technical issues and reports concerns to Parliament.

sections.content: In an essay on controls, ensure you distinguish clearly between parliamentary controls (the role of Parliament itself) and judicial controls (the role of the courts). A strong answer will analyse the effectiveness of each type of control, not just describe them.

The courts control delegated legislation through the doctrine of 'ultra vires' (acting beyond one's powers). Any piece of delegated legislation can be challenged in the High Court via judicial review. If the court finds it to be ultra vires, it will be declared void and have no legal effect. There are two main types. Procedural ultra vires occurs when the correct procedure laid down in the enabling Act has not been followed, as seen in the Aylesbury Mushroom Case (1972), where failure to consult the Mushroom Growers' Association rendered a regulation void. Substantive ultra vires is when the content of the delegated legislation goes beyond the powers granted. A subset of this is unreasonableness, where a decision is so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could have made it (the Wednesbury principle from Associated Provincial Picture Houses v Wednesbury Corporation (1948)).

items: Judicial control is exercised through judicial review in the High Court.
The core principle is 'ultra vires' – acting beyond the powers granted by the enabling Act.
Procedural Ultra Vires: The correct process for making the law was not followed (e.g., Aylesbury Mushroom Case).
Substantive Ultra Vires: The content of the law exceeds the powers granted, including if it is 'Wednesbury unreasonable'.

sections.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of delegated legislation. [15 marks]
sections.solution:
**Advantages:** Saves **Parliamentary time**; **technical detail** by experts; **flexibility** to amend quickly; **local bylaws** suit local needs; **emergency** Orders in Council (e.g. foot-and-mouth).

**Disadvantages:** **Undemocratic** — unelected ministers; **lack of scrutiny** — many SIs pass unnoticed (negative resolution); **volume** hard to monitor; **sub-delegation** concerns.

**Controls:** **Parliament** — committees (Scrutiny Committee), affirmative/negative procedures; **Courts** — *Agricultural Fisheries case* (procedural ultra vires), *Attorney-General v Fulham* (substantive).

**Conclusion:** **Necessary and efficient** but requires **strong controls** to maintain accountability.

faq.q: Is delegated legislation considered 'real' law with the same authority as an Act of Parliament?
faq.a: Yes and no. Delegated legislation is legally binding and has the full force of law, so in that sense, it is 'real' law that you must obey. However, it is subordinate to Acts of Parliament. An Act of Parliament can override any piece of delegated legislation, and the courts can declare delegated legislation void if it is 'ultra vires'. The courts cannot do this to an Act of Parliament due to the doctrine of Parliamentary Sovereignty.

faq.q: What is the practical difference between the affirmative and negative resolution procedures?
faq.a: The key difference is the default outcome. With the negative procedure, the default is that the law passes unless Parliament actively stops it. This requires less parliamentary time and scrutiny. With the affirmative procedure, the default is that the law fails unless Parliament actively approves it. This forces a debate and a vote, ensuring a higher level of scrutiny for more significant or controversial powers.

faq.q: If delegated legislation is made by unelected people like civil servants, doesn't that make it undemocratic?
faq.a: This is a major criticism and a key point for evaluation. The argument is that law-making power is passed from elected MPs to unelected civil servants or ministers. However, the democratic justification is that the power is delegated by a democratically elected Parliament, the minister responsible is accountable to Parliament, and Parliament retains ultimate control through the enabling Act and scrutiny procedures. Whether these controls are effective enough to counter the democratic deficit is a central debate.

comparisonTable.caption: Comparison of Acts of Parliament and Delegated Legislation

cells: The Crown in Parliament (House of Commons, House of Lords, and the Monarch).
Government ministers, the Privy Council, local authorities, or public bodies.
Parliamentary Sovereignty. Parliament is the supreme law-making body.
An 'Enabling' or 'Parent' Act of Parliament, which delegates the power.
Slow and lengthy process involving multiple readings, committee stages, and debates in both Houses.
Quick to create and amend, often with limited parliamentary debate (e.g., negative resolution).
Cannot be challenged or declared invalid by the courts. The courts must apply the law as written.
Can be challenged in the courts via judicial review and declared void if found to be 'ultra vires'.
Can cover any topic and make broad changes to the law.
Limited to the specific powers and purposes laid out in the enabling Act.
